where,
N t
denotes number of NPs at any interval t-second, and
μ bf
represents dynamic viscosity of the base-fluid.

Materials termed as surfactants (e.g. PVP) are utilized to lower liquid surface tension, which is a major factor in phase change distribution, see Fig. 9. Both the liquid–liquid and the solid–liquid systems benefit from this [225]. Dispersant are surfactants that are used to stabilize solid particles within a liquid. Surfactants have been shown in numerous studies to improve the stability and effectiveness of NFs. It was shown how surfactants, such as SDS, PVP, and CTAB, affected the stability and thermophysical characteristics of hybrid NFs [226]. Improved viscosity and stability were established by the high surfactant concentrations; PVP surfactant showed the best stability values [227]. Xia et al. [228] explored the influence of surfactants on stability of Al
2
O
3
/water NFs, showing the crucial role of surfactants in dispersing particles and enhancing stability. Cacua et al., [229] examined the stability of Al
2
O
3
-water NFs using different surfactants, showing the significant influence of surfactants on stability. Chaturvedi and Co. [230] examined the role of surfactants in single-step silica NFs for enhanced oil recovery applications, demonstrating that surfactant treatment enhanced stability as well as resistance to salt-induced agglomeration, making them appropriate for oil-field applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity and stability of the plasmonic nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
The stability factor is considered as a significant indicator in determining the thermal conductivity of NFs [231,232]. The particles interact with the environmental medium to their robust capability when they are uniformly distributed throughout the base-fluid and efficiently transfer heat without clumping or agglomerating. This long-term dispersion increases the possibility that NPs will stay near one another, establishing effective channels for ongoing heat transmission [233,234]. Zhang et al. [73] used various surfactants and surface modification techniques to investigate the stability and heat conductivity of TiO
2
/water NFs. They found that whereas NFs made with modified NPs comprising gamma-glycidoxypropyltrimethoxysilane (GPTMS) demonstrated outstanding stability, those using unmodified NPs and SDS demonstrated moderate stability. Furthermore, they noted that thermal conductivity improved with temperature and NP concentration, with GPTMS-modified NFs demonstrating greater long-term consistency and thermal conductivity. Jin et al. [235] synthesized metal NP-filled carbon nanotubes (i.e., Cu@CNT, Ag@CNT) and examined their viscosity, thermal conductivity, and dispersion stability in both ethylene glycol and water.

It was explored by comparing hybrid NFs, such as Ag@CNT and Cu@CNT-based working fluids, the former demonstrated reduced viscosity and high heat conductivity. These working fluids also demonstrated remarkable enhancement in thermal conductivity and good colloidal stability up to 6 months period. The synthesis of NFs based on TiO
2
NPs using different surfactants and surface modification methods was obtained to obtain high optothermal properties. The TiO
2
-based NFs was treated with tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAH) and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTS) to obtain high dispersion stability [212]. Due to better particles adherence, NFs treated with APTS displayed enhanced viscosity. When both temperature and NPs concentration increased, these NFs provided enhancement in thermal conductivity. Meanwhile, Bao et al. [236] evaluated the efficiency of EG-based NFs containing single-layer and multilayer Ti
3
C
2
Tx MXene nanosheets. They exhibited robust enhancements in thermal conductivity, such as single-layer Ti
3
C
2
Tx-EG-based NFs, illustrating lower viscosity and higher stability over a 30-day period.

When compared to ethylene glycol (EG) alone, the addition of 5 vol% of single-layer and multilayer Ti
3
C
2
Tx to EG significantly increased thermal conductivity by 64.9% and 53.1%, respectively. MXene outperformed Gr and multi-walled CNTs, which needed 0.1 vol% to reach comparable viscounties, in terms of viscosity at just 1 vol%, despite the significant improvement in heat conductivity.These results highlight the potential of MXene nanosheets as extremely effective heat transfer enhancers. Furthermore, Jin et al. [237] used GR and GR/MXene to create novel NFs that improved thermal conductivity. According to their results, MXene improved the dispersion stability of GR NFs and decreased viscosity while maintaining thermal conductivity. With addition of MXene, the heat transfer efficiency of these NFs approached that of GR NFs, surpassing that of water.

Meanwhile, Mukherjee and colleagues [238] investigated the increase of thermal conductivity and heat transfer performance in Al
2
O
3
and TiO
2
NFs, revealing significant improvements in stable NFs. Al
2
O
3
NFs showed the most promising results, with a maximum 44% of enhancement in thermal conductivity, and a maximum 21% increase in transient heat transfer performance compared to their base-fluids. Ezekwem and Dare [239] synthesized SiC NFs, noting better stability in SiC/DW NFs over SiC/EG NFs.Their findings demonstrated that both working fluids indicated exceptional improvement in thermal and electrical conductivities, containing SiC-EG and SiC-DW NFs. Further, they showed the maximum thermal conductivity of SiC-EG and SiC-DW NFs, increased up to 25% and 16%, respectively. Meanwhile, Qamar et al., [240] developed ZnO-based NFs, exhibiting the stabilizing agents prolonged suspension stability up to 60 days. The remarkable enhancements, such as 16.75% increase in viscosity as well as, 23.70% rise in thermal conductivity, were examined in working fluids stabilized with sodium hexametaphosphate. The authors also presented that viscosity and thermal conductivity demonstrated enhancement with particle concentration. Similarly, Gao et al., [241] elucidated the durability of GrO-Al
2
O
3
hybrid working fluids for energy harvesting and storage applications, recognizing pH, ultrasonic power, dispersant, and duration as crucial parameters. Moreover, the temperature, and concentration affected thermal conductivity, and stability maintained over 60 days under optimal conditions. In another study, Sandhya et al., [242] presented a detailed analysis of Gr-based working fluids, underscoring Gr strong stability and higher thermal conductivity. Their findings further discussed the improvements in both features with enhancing volume concentration and temperature, demonstrating the potentials in clean and cost-effective energy systems.

However, Sunil and colleagues [243] evaluated dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity of colloidal Ag NPs dispersions, underscoring their superior performance compared to conventional fluids in context of viscosity and thermal conductivity. Meanwhile, Urmi et al., [244] explored 40% hybrid NFs (TiO
2
-Al
2
O
3
), exhibiting enhanced viscosity as well as thermal conductivity relative to the conventional fluids. Notably, the NFs exhibited a remarkable 40.86% increase in thermal conductivity at 80
°
C and 0.1% volume concentration. Conversely, Ma et al., [245] investigated MXene/water NFs and demonstrated the significant improvement in stability, thermal conductivity and viscosity obtained by introducing anhydrous sodium citrate and extending the ultrasonication period. The authors showed a remarkable 27.41% increase in thermal behavior at specific concentration of 0.1% weight and temperature of 60
°
C. Finally, Ma et al., [226] presented the influence of numerous surfactants for hybrid NFs, evaluating that PVP surfactant indicated outstanding stability, with minimal sedimentation examined over a period of 25 days. Overall, these findings highlight the significant improvements in stability and thermal conductivity via different combinations of NP compositions and surfactant additives.

Makishima et al. [246] concluded that formation of hybrid metals occurred, when two or more materials are mixed in such a way as to generate a new chemical bond. However, in practice, “hybrid nanofluids” are created through the homogeneous blending of two or more metals, resulting in a uniformly dispersed phase. A significant improvement in heat transfer properties, such as thermophysical features and rheological properties are revealed through comparative analysis with single-component NFs. The researchers investigated that hybrid NFs performed better than single or standard NFs in terms of heat transfer rate, even though they involved heat generation, chemical reactions and thermal radiation. As reported by Farooq et al., [21], hybrid NPs-based colloids illustrated huge potential for solar energy harvesting in DASC, with Au NRs chains indicating versatile broad band spectral absorption as well as, potential for orientation control. They exhibited that hybrid NPs configurations increased solar absorption in thermal collectors containing tetramer Au NRs assemblies, revealing remarkable temperature enhancement.

Sidik et al., [247] organized an in-depth investigation into the hybrid NFs, focusing on nanocomposites including GOFe
3
O
4
, and γ
Al 2
O 3
/MWCNT, and activated CGr. Their findings encompassed techniques for evaluating stability, preparation methodologies for hybrid type NFs, as well as their thermophysical properties, along with an overview of emerging advancement, challenges and utilization in heat-transfer. Key outcomes indicated that the synthesis technique potentially influenced hybrid NFs performance and certain fluid properties exhibited improvement with elevated temperature and concentration. Nevertheless, it is pointed out that the characteristics in context of hybrid NFs from different studies could alter. Similarly, another research investigated thermal conductivity of hybrid NFs, analyzing preparation/fabrication techniques, challenges, and key observations. Further, the investigation revealed a correlation between the thermal conductivity, particle concentration and temperature. Nevertheless, the stability emerged as a remarkable factor influencing applicability of hybrid NFs across several industries. Furthermore, the complex manufacturing techniques and criticality of choosing suitable nanocomposites further shed light on the need of careful considerations in this domain [248].

Several studies conducted by various researchers have indicated that hybrid NFs, such as alumina-graphene (Al
2
O
3
+GnP) and alumina-multiwalled carbon nanotubes (Al
2
O
3
+MWCNTs) at a 1% concentration of NPs, as well as paraffin- and soybean based NFs with 8 wt.% of MoS
2
NPs, exhibit superior thermo-physical, lubrication, tribological, and wetting properties across different machining mechanisms. Notably, NFs containing CNTs and Al
2
O
3
demonstrate minimal values for particular grinding energy, cutting temperature and surface roughness, when compared to flooded or dry methods [249]. Further, research has shown that NFs and hybrid NFs surpass other cooling and lubrication methods in effectiveness [247].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal conductivity and NP size of plasmonic nanofluids

ce:section.ce:para:
Invaluable understanding into thermal conductivity and NP size-dependent insights of NFs could be achieved via diverse array of experimental analysis. These studies provide comprehensive understanding of how affecting parameters, such as NP size, fluid compositions, and concentration, influence thermal performance of working fluids. The study has exhibited that efficient thermal conductivity of working fluids is greatly affected by size, with tiny particles (<50 nm) generally revealing optimal heat transfer capabilities [250,251]. Additionally, an experimental analysis has highlighted the significance of numerous factors like thickness of liquid nanolayer as well as, NP surface properties in designing heat conductivity. Such as, Wang et al. [252] showed the measurement of thermal conductivities in fluids with CuO and Al
2
O
3
NPs suspended in various base-fluids. Further, their findings indicated improvement in thermal conductivity compared to traditional base-fluids, establishing a correlation between decrease NP size and increase thermal conductivity. Similarly, Farooq et al., [61] explored TiN NFs with different sizes, exhibiting high solar weighted absorption coefficients and temperature increases under illumination, with significant thermal efficiency compared to Au-based counterparts in DASC. A comprehensive analysis of systematic research regarding
A m
factor and thermal conductivity and several other parameters can be seen in Table 3 and Table 5, respectively.

Recently, a plethora of literature has been dedicated to revealing the impact of particle size on thermal conductivity of NFs, an integral aspect for several industrial applications. The researchers focus to explain how these variables (like composition, volume concentration, and particle size) affect the performance of thermal conductivity in working fluids. Such outcomes not only assist the optimization of particle performance in heat transfer applications but also make invaluable understanding into their size-related behavior. A comprehensive analysis of NP size and their influence on thermal conductivity can be depicted in Table 5. For instance, Qu et al., [253] analyzed the optothermal energy conversion characteristics and optical absorption traits of GrO water NFs, exhibiting that decreasing the size of GrO sheet or enhancing its mass-fraction increased optical spectral absorption capacity. Further, they also showed an optimal concentration of GrO, binding to sheet size and resulting temperature increments. Besides, numerical analysis presented the influence of irradiation angle on temperature increase, stressing experimental outcomes. Similarly, Lee et al., [254] demonstrated viscosity of NFs, containing C-NPs, such as GrO and graphite altered with oleic acid, and decreased GrO to estimate their thermal behavior. In context of size effects and C-NPs affinity for base-fluid, the interaction between NPs with tuned lateral diameters caused NFs dynamic viscosity to reduce their thermal trait.

The investigation of metal oxide (MO) NFs highlights the significance of careful parameter selection, involving NP volume-fraction and stabilizers, in improving thermo-physical traits. Overall, these outcomes facilitate invaluable understanding for accurately tuned NFs compositions and employ their improved heat-transfer characteristics across multiple industrial utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Direct correlation between particle size and effective thermal conductivity
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of particle agglomeration, stabilizing methods, and volume fraction ( ϕ ranging from 0.00025 to 1%) were examined on the effective thermal conductivity (
k eff
) of Au NPs dispersed in water and a water-methanol combination [109], as presented in Table 4 . The NF, Au NPs/water (with particle diameter
d NP
= 40 nm and volume fraction ϕ = 11%), stabilized by the EGMUDE surfactant, exhibited a maximum increase in
k eff
of 1.4%. Additionally, the increase in particle diameter from 2 nm to 40 nm was correlated with a considerable rise in
k eff
, as depicted in Fig. 12 . An exclusive study provides an-depth analysis of viscosity correlations confining theoretical, and experimental approaches. Analyzing approximately 1200 experimental and 4000 theoretical datasets across 50 types of NFs, with particle
d NP
ranging from 2 to 300 nm and temperatures between 273 K and 333 K, a novel correlation was introduced. The findings unveil NFs with volume fractions up to 10%, offering valuable insights into viscosity behavior across various conditions and particle sizes. Nanofluids comprising 25% water and 75% ethylene glycol demonstrate superior stability compared to counterparts with 75% water and 25% ethylene glycol [255]. Furthermore, the increase in particle size significantly affects transport features such as viscosity, and thermal conductivity, alongside parameters such as Nusselt number, Biot number, and convection heat-transfer coefficient, highlighting the substantial influence of particle-fluid interaction. The impact of tuning
d NP
on
k eff
of SiC NPs dispersed in
H 2
O was comprehensively examined by Xie et al. [256]. Two NFs types were investigated: one with cylindrical shaped NPs (
d NP
= 600 nm) and the other with spherical shaped particles (
d NP
= 26 nm). Results revealed an important change in
k eff
with decreasing
d NP
at higher particle concentrations. At ϕ = 4.2%, a maximum increase of 22.9% in
k eff
was observed for the NF with
d NP
= 600 nm. Additionally, it is explored that impact of
d NP
on the
k eff
of aqueous NFs containing SiO
2
, TiO
2
, Al
2
O
3
, and ZrO
2
NPs with particle-sizes ranging from 10 to 100 nm, 70 to 150 nm, 40 to 150 nm, and 44 to 105 nm, respectively, at a volume fraction of 2%. The findings demonstrated that at room temperature, the
k eff
of each NF exhibited a rising trend with increasing
d NP
. However, the rate of
k eff
enhancement varied among different NF samples, highlighting inconsistent behavior across the reported NFs [257]. Moreover, Chen and Co. [258] investigated the impact of NPs size on the
k eff
of SiO
2
-water NF. They observed that at a volume fraction ( ϕ ) of 16%,
k eff
increased as
d NP
enhance from 100 to 280 nm. Another study showed that increasing the size of spherical gold nanoparticles from 5 nm to 50 nm led to a red-shift in the LSPR resonance peak, thereby enhancing the sensor's sensitivity and overall performance [170], see Fig. 13 . The authors attributed this tendency to higher heat resistance at the particle liquid interface resulting from the larger
d NP
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inverse correlation between particle size and effective thermal conductivity
ce:section.ce:para:
Ambreen et al. [305] explored how NP size affected pressure drop and heat-transfer in working fluid during laminar forced convection in microchannels with constant heat flow. They considered two particles weight concentrations (0.1% and 2%) at Reynolds numbers (1000, 1200, and 2000), employing Al
2
O
3
and TiO
2
NPs in aqueous NFs. Their findings show an inverse relationship between particle diameter and friction factor, as well as convective heat transmission. Smaller particles exhibit higher heat transfer coefficients due to their better distribution along the channel and larger effective surface area. This effect is more attributed to higher particle concentrations, with maximum difference of 11% in heat transmission and 20% in friction factor observed between particle size of 20 nm and 200 nm at 2% concentration. Similarly, the authors [306] examined the
k eff
of Al-Cu/water NFs (0.2% ≤ ϕ ≤ 2.0%) and observed a significant decrease in
k eff
as
d NP
increase from 10 to 90 nm. This tendency was associated to increase stability, mobility, and specific surface area of smaller NPs, leading to improved interphase interactions. Manna et al., [307] observed a remarkable enhancements in thermal conductivity through induction of silicon carbide (SiC) NP-dispersed water-based NFs containing NP concentrations of over 0.1%. Remarkably, even with just 0.1% NP volume, the NF exhibited a remarkable improvement in thermal conductivity, reaching up to 12%, as determined by transient hot wire device, highlighting an inverse correlation between conductivity and particle size. These findings underscore the suitability of ceramic NPs, such as SiC, for maintaining dispersion stability in NFs, particularly in single-phase closed-loop heat-transfer applications. Liu and colleagues [259] investigated
k eff
of Cu-water NFs ( ϕ < 0.2%) with various NP shapes (needle, square, and spherical) and sizes (50–250 nm). They found that for NPs of the same shape, the
k eff
exhibited an inverse relationship with
d NP
. Chon and co-authors [262] assessed the enhancement in
k eff
of Al
2
O
3
-water NF by increasing the volume fraction (1% ≤ ϕ ≤ 4%) and temperature range (294 K–344 K), while reducing the NP size (11–150 nm). They emphasized the significant influence of particle Brownian motion on k
eff
, as evidenced by the strong dependence of
k eff
on both NP
d NP
and temperature. In another study, two types of NFs—SiO
2
and Al
2
O
3
—in de-ionized water were investigated [308]. The experimental setup contained a rectangular cavity along with heated horizontal walls, creating a T-gradient across the fluids layer. Interferograms were employed captured to examine the transient evolution of the thermal diffusion field with respect to time. The transient heat-diffusion equation was resolved analytically as the forward model, considering the estimated thermal diffusivity.

Li et al. [309] explored a nonlinear increase in the
k eff
of Al
2
O
3
NF with the reduction of
d NP
(36 nm, 47 nm) and the enhancement of T (300 K < T < 310 K) and ϕ (0.5% ≤ ϕ ≤ 6.0%). The fluctuation of
d NP
dependent
k eff
was more considerable with greater T and ϕ . Furthermore, A plethora of studies discussed the impact of agglomeration size, and highlighted the significant role of fragmentation-induced reduction in particle size in enhancing
d NP
[269]. For instance, Kim and team. [310] found an inverse relation between mean diameter of ZnO and TiO
2
NPs suspended in H
2
O and EG and their respective
k eff
values. A decrease in particle diameter (i.e.
d NP
= 95 nm, 145 nm, 210 nm) correlates with an increase in the
k eff
of TiO
2
NF ( ϕ = 0.60%). It is also reported that enhancement degree in thermal conductivity rises with higher NP concentrations and smaller sizes. The peak enhancement reaches approximately 48% at concentration of 0.00026 vol.% and an average NP size of 21 nm [126].

Overall, aforementioned studies consistently present a remarkable correlation between
k eff
and
d NP
in NFs. Generally, reducing
d NP
governs to an increase in
k eff
, a trend observed across several suspension media and NFs compositions, indicating its widespread applicability. Several factors, including agglomeration, fragmentation-induced size reduction, and NP features, influence
k eff
. Understanding these correlations is pivotal for refining NF compositions and exploiting their enhanced thermal properties across diverse industrial applications. Further, research into the mechanisms driving the relation between NP size and thermal conductivity is imperative to generative more accurate predictive models for NF behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-linear relation between particle size and effective thermal conductivity
ce:section.ce:para:
The
k eff
of Al
2
O
3
/distilled water NFs was evaluated via steady-state method performed by Li et al. [309], using NP sizes of 36 nm and 47 nm. Experiments covered volume fractions from 0.5% to 6.0% and temperatures between 27
°
C and 37
°
C. Their findings revealed a nonlinear relationship between
k eff
enhancement and temperature, volume fraction, and NP size, indicating that an increase in any of these parameters led to enhanced thermal conductivity. It was the influence of particle size variation on the NFs’
k eff
, with the most significant enhancement observed at a volume fraction between 2% and 4% and around 32
°
C. Further, some NFs exhibit remarkable thermal properties, such as temperature- and size-sensitive thermal conductivity, unusually high thermal conductivity at low NPs concentrations, a non-linear correlation between concentration and thermal conductivity, and a threefold rise in critical heat-flux at approximately 10 ppm particle concentration. These intriguing thermal transport behaviors surpass conventional macroscopic models of suspensions, making NFs a subject of significant scientific intrigue. Consequently, numerous theories and approaches have been demonstrated to elucidate these unexpected and fascinating thermal characteristics of NFs [311].

Further, it was observed that the
k eff
of Al
2
O
3
in three base-fluids increases with NP size within the range of 15 nm ≤
d NP
≤ 60.5 nm [270]. However, a different trend in
k eff
was noted for larger particle sizes (60.5 nm ≤
d NP
≤ 302 nm). The study explained that an increase in
d NP
beyond the phonon mean-free path results in a larger interfacial area for NP-liquid interactions, leading to an enhancement in
k eff
. Conversely, when the particle size approaches or falls below the phonon mean-free path,
k eff
decreases due to phonon scattering at the particle boundary. Further, researchers explored that the size-dependent
k eff
of room temperature Al
2
O
3
/water and Al
2
O
3
/EG NFs [312]. They observed that
k eff
exhibited an increasing tendency with increasing
d NP
in 2 nm ≤
d NP
< 50 nm range, which was attributed to phonon scattering at the particle-liquid interface. However,
k eff
reached a limited value at constant ( ϕ ) for NP sizes between 50 nm <
d NP
≤ 282 nm.

Several theoretical frameworks, spanning from Maxwell classical models [313] to Hamilton and Crosser (HC) [314], and Wasp [315], have delineated the
k eff
of particle-liquid composites. These conventional models predominantly account for diffusive heat-transport in both phases, operating on continuum-level phenomenological formulations. However, advancements in understanding have underscored the influence of particle diffusivity, Brownian motion-induced nano-convections, fluid layering at the particle-liquid interface, and particle chain architectures on the enhancement of
k eff
in NFs.

In order to evaluate the
k eff
, the Maxwell and Hamilton and Crosser equations can be explored as:
(32)
κ eff
=
κ p
+ 2
κ f

,

Several studies have proposed that the primary mechanism governing the
k eff
of NFs is the fluid convection induced by particle Brownian motion. Utilizing the kinetic theory and considering different methods of heat transfer between similar and dissimilar phases, researchers developed a theoretical
k eff
model [300]. Further, Xuan et al. [299] modified the Maxwell model to incorporate the aggregation structures of NPs and their Brownian motion, yielding an equation for the
k eff
. The model encompasses the influences of NP aggregates, their morphology, and physical properties, and the characteristics of base-fluid. Their research revealed direct and inverse correlations between
k eff
and temperature as well as particle diameter
d NP
, respectively.

A plethora of literature on NF based thermal conductivity have elucidated the variables impacting this crucial characteristic through a variety of models and experimental investigations. In order to account for non-local heat transfer effects, Shaker and his team [316] suggested an Extended Maxwell model, which they demonstrated to be accurate in predicting changes in thermal conductivity in alumina NFs. Using the cell model technique, Mehta and Co.[317] developed a novel predictive model that effectively captured patterns observed in experimental data at various temperatures, concentrations, and particle sizes. Introducing a phase mixture model that distinguishes between NFs and nano-crystalline solids, Pabst et al. [318] provided insights into the function of border phase characteristics. Selected studies on on thermal conductivity by computational modeling/numerical simulations are shown in Table 6
.

The relation between NP size and thermal conductivity is a focal point of recent research, particularly concerning NFs and nanocrystalline solids. Pabst et al. [318] demonstrated a phase mixture modeling method to elucidate distinct thermal conductivity trends observed in these materials, attributing them to the presence of a boundary phase acting either as a barrier or a thermal bridge. Meanwhile, Ueki et al. [319] delved into the effects of sedimentation and aggregation diameter on nanoparticle suspension fluids’ thermal conductivity, revealing unique behavior patterns in suspensions of graphite and carbon black, driven by surface characteristics and particle shape transformation. Meanwhile, Lee and Co. [114] explored the
k eff
of ZnO NF using one-step physical method, demonstrating the importance of NP clustering and aggregation on the observed temperature-dependent feature and experimental discrepancies with theoretical models.

Based on 1065 experimental datasets compiled from 36 literature sources, Khalifeh et al. [320] developed precise artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to predict how aggregation affects the thermal conductivity of various NFs. Among the AI models tested, the cascade feed-forward backpropagation neural network proved superior, achieving a remarkable average relative deviation of 1.37%. Meanwhile, Namboori and team [321] utilized molecular dynamics simulation to assess the thermal conductivity of NFs, focusing on NPs such as TiO
2
, CuO, and Al
2
O
3
. Their findings indicated that integrating NPs enhances the fluid effective thermal conductivity, thereby enhancing heat transfer efficiency and potentially reducing pollution while increasing energy efficiency. Zendehboudi et al. [322] employed various techniques to simulate effective thermal conductivity of working fluids under diverse conditions. Their Multilayer Perceptron-Artificial Neural Network (MLP-ANN) algorithms, with a relative root-mean squared error of 1.534%, and coefficient of determination of 0.994, demonstrated superior accuracy compared to previous approaches.

The relationship between effective thermal conductivity (ETC) of NFs and NP properties has been the subject of recent investigations, employing diverse methodologies to uncover significant insights. Tahmooressi et al. [323] delved into the impact of NP geometry on NF thermal conductivity using mesoscale numerical simulations via the Lattice Boltzmann method. Their findings revealed a direct correlation between ETC and NP volume fraction, with particular emphasis on the aspect ratio influence. Conversely, Maleki et al. [324] demonstrated the efficacy of artificial intelligence, particularly Multilayer Perceptron-Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models, in accurately predicting thermal conductivity containing ZnO particles, surpassing alternative methods in performance and precision. In his comprehensive examination of thermal conductivity in NFs, Das [325] underscored the importance of synthesizing stable NFs and discussed the merits and drawbacks of hybrid NFs. Furthermore, Feng et al. [326] proposed a probability model for NP size distribution. The effective thermal conductivity model-based on fractal geometry theory was developed, validated through Monte Carlo simulations. These models exhibited strong alignment with experimental data, offering valuable insights into the determinants of thermal conductivity.

Meanwhile, Zhang and Li. [327] conducted a detailed review of existing studies on the heat transfer capabilities of NFs, highlighting inconsistencies and uncertainties in theoretical analyses, experimental results, and computational models. Their aim was to provide guidance for future research on NF heat transfer performance and propose novel approaches to address challenges in this field.

The reported differences in thermal conductivity enhancement among studies investigating the effect of particle size may stem from various factors. Firstly, variations in the synthesis techniques used to produce NPs could lead to differences in size distribution and surface properties, influencing their interaction with the base-fluid and overall thermal conductivity enhancement. Discrepancies in results may also arise from differences due to experimental conditions like pressure, temperature and sample preparation techniques. Furthermore, the choice of base-fluid and its characteristics, including viscosity and thermal conductivity, could impact NP dispersion and consequently affect thermal conductivity enhancement. Addressing these factors and working towards standardization in future research endeavors can help improve result consistency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of particle shape on nanofluid performance
Particle shape and thermal conductivity of plasmonic nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
To manipulate thermal conductivity of NFs, researchers have developed hybrid NFs by combining multiple NPs, a promising solution. Particularly, attention has shifted towards ternary NFs, demonstrating enhanced heat transfer properties and thermal conductivity because of inclusion of three solid NPs within a conventional fluid. The study investigated synthesis methodologies, characterizations, stability assessments, and thermophysical properties of ternary NFs, showing the significance of NP types, sizes, mixing ratios, and base-fluids. Furthermore, ternary NFs are depicted to strongly improve thermal conductivity, and heat-transfer features of base-fluids [329]. It was examined how adding spherical and cylindrical alumina NPs affected the thermophysical characteristics of molten salt [330]. According to experimental results, adding 1% alumina nanoparticles increased heat conductivity by 16% for cylindrical particles and 12% for spherical particles. Nevertheless, this improvement in heat conductivity was combined with a 37% increase in fluid viscosity for spherical-shaped NPs and 25% increase for cylindrical NPs. Trodi et al. [331] performed detailed analysis to show the influence of AR and morphology on spontaneous convection in a square cavity filled with Al
2
O
3
-based NF. It was examined three different NPs shapes: oblate spheroid, spherical, and prolate spheroid, with varying aspect ratios. The work indicated that heat-transfer characteristics were potentially affected by the shape and ARs of the NPs. Specifically, heat transfer was most enhanced by oblate spheroid NPs. Similarly, Murshed and Co. [261] employed spherical and non-spherical shaped NPs to synthesize TiO
2
/water NFs and examined spherical NPs predict lesser values than rod-shaped NPs. Further, Jeong et al. [332] observed that NFs containing ZnO particles with rectangular shapes exhibited 5.9% high values of thermal conductivity compared to those containing spherical NPs.

The correlation applies to NFs containing various shapes of boehmite Al
2
O
3
NPs dispersed in ethylene glycol-water mixture (50:50) [333].
(34)
κ eff
=
κ f
1 +
C k shape

C k surface

κ f
1 + ϕ
C k
where,
C k shape
and
C k surface
represent thermal conductivity due to shape and surface resistance.

Researchers employed the Brinkman model [334] to calculate the dynamic viscosity of NFs containing spherical-shaped NPs, given as:
(35)

κ f

For non-spherical NP, dynamic viscosity is explored, given as [333]:
(36)

A 1

where,
A 1
,
A 2
represent particles shapes.

Ghadikolaei et al. [335] explored the influence of NP shape on 3D squeezing flow in a hybrid NF. They explored three NP shapes: cube, cylindrical, and platelet. The study revealed that increasing shape factor established higher heat-transfer rates, and reduced temperatures. Platelet-shaped NPs, with the highest shape factor, showed the highest heat-transfer rate. Benkhedda et al. [336] investigated how NP shape affects heat transfer efficiency in hybrid NFs flowing through a tube. Water, TiO
2
NF, and Ag/TiO
2
hybrid NFs were investigated. This investigation included four different particle shapes: platelets, spherical, cylindrical, and blade. The maximum heat-transfer rate was shown by blade-shaped NPs, which were followed by spherical, platelet, and cylindrical nanoparticles. Furthermore, the NPs with the highest friction factor were platelet-shaped. Aziz et al. [337] investigated radiative heat-exchange, angled magnetic-field, heat-transfer, Joule heating, and NP shape factor in a study focusing on heat-transfer, and entropy generation of Maxwell hybrid NFs across a porous stretched disk. Comparing Cu/EG and Fe
3
O 4 –Cu/EG NFs studies, they found improved heat-transfer capability with hybrid particles. To obtain the maximum thermal efficiency, NPs morphology and heat transfer rates were illustrated to be negatively correlated. These results supported by other investigations underscored the fact how difficult it was to consider NP shape into account. The investigations on PCMs with NPs demonstrated that some shapes—such as carbon nanotubes and nanofibers—performed better at thermal properties due to special geometrical configuration. These outcomes showed the significance of understanding the correlation between morphology and thermal conductivity for effective design and utilization across numerous thermal management systems.

The variations in thermal conductivity enhancement attributed to particle shape may stem from observed alterations in particle surface area and morphology. When compared to other geometries, spherical NPs often have a lower surface area-to-volume ratio, which promotes poor base-fluid interactions. However, under specific circumstances, non-spherical NPs, such as rods or platelets, may show improved alignment and packing within the fluid, enhancing heat transfer pathways and amplifying the boost of thermal conductivity. Upon examining the contradictory results from the examined research, a number of things become apparent as possible causes of the observed disparities. First, conflicting results may be caused by variations in NP synthesis techniques and experimental settings, such as variations in temperature, pressure, and sample preparation techniques. Secondly, variations in numerical predictions might arise from differences in the numerical models and boundary conditions employed in simulations, particularly regarding the treatment of fluid properties and particle shapes. Primarily, the contradictory results may arise by modifications in NP fabrication techniques and experimental settings, such as fluctuations in pressure, temperature, and sample preparation techniques. Secondly, the discrepancies in modeling predictions can cause through differences in numerical calculations and boundary conditions used in simulations. The numerical solutions can tune because of differences in assumed fluid characteristics and NP morphologies, and how the model incorporates internal heat generation and radiative heat. There are several limitations in existing models, like MUltiple-SIze-Group (MUSIG) model, with respect to the treatment of NP assembly can affect at bubble surfaces and their influence on interfacial interactions. To determine the discrepancies between numerical and experimental findings, a reliable experimental techniques and advancements in numerical models can assist to mitigate contradictory results, necessitating advance knowledge in the field of NF research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape dependent solar weighted absorption coefficient
ce:section.ce:para:
Investigating the application of NFs to enhance solar thermal conversion systems suggests a viable approach to the development of renewable energy. Targeting Fe
3
O 4 NPs with varying specific concentrations and geometries has shown promising results thus far. The blade-based working fluids demonstrated a 96.2% spectrum absorption at a concentration of 0.02 vol% and a penetration distance of 1 cm. Nevertheless, the spherical and octahedral NPs showed reduced absorption rates in the base fluid, ranging from 87.2% to 93.5%, respectively. After illuminating one hour of light, the blade-based NFs at specific concentration (0.02%) obtained the highest solar thermal energy conversion performance, reaching over 82% [338]. Currently, GrO-based NFs have demonstrated their potential to increase the efficiency of solar collectors, employing non-Newtonian formulations showing their remarkable performance than that of Newtonian counterparts [339]. Meanwhile, the findings of Gr-based NPs suspended in different base-fluids have exhibited their ability on improving the effectiveness of flat-plate thermal collectors under various operating parameters [340]. Overall, these outcomes stress the potential of NF-based technology to enhance optothermal conversion phenomenon, leading for improvement in both sustainability and efficiency. Moreover, in another study, the researchers used three types of NPs concentrations, such as 0.005, 0.01, and 0.02 vol%) for blade, spherical, and octahedral shapes, respectively. To prepare these NF, the anionic surfactant and ultrasonic treatment have been used to obtain small particle size distribution as well as durable stability. They showed remarkable optical absorption within visible range, and the absorption spectral band increases as NP concentrations enhance. Based on their findings, the blade-shaped particles indicated the optimal
A m
factor, performing better than octahedral and spherical shapes. At a concentration of 0.02 vol%, blade-NFs absorbed 96.2% of solar intensity, surpassing spherical and octahedral NFs. Thermal tests managed in bath direct solar collectors revealed that 0.02% blade-NFs, after one hour of light exposure, achieved the maximum solar-thermal conversion efficiency (82%) and produced a bulk temperature rise 2.31 times higher than water [338].

The DASC thermal efficiency can be altered with promising outcomes on introducing transition metal nitrides (e.g. ZrN, TiN, and HfN) NPs in base-fluids. These nanomaterials unraveled promising properties matched properly with solar radiation spectrum, indicating lower scattering efficiency and optimal spectral absorption. In comparison to the reference material, Au NF (spherical shaped), collectors employing these NFs demonstrated enhancements in thermal performance ranging from 36.5% to 50.4% with optical efficiency (
A m
= 95%). Moreover, under low volume fraction conditions, transition metal nitride-based DASCs achieved higher outlet temperatures and exergy efficiencies, rendering them suitable for solar-related heating technologies [46]. Moreover, an important development investigated by Farooq and Co., [16] from the induction of Fe-doped Au NS in base-fluid for solar energy harvesting. Computational modeling calculations revealed that Fe-doped Au NS-based NFs presented significantly higher
A m
factor in comparison to Au NS-based working fluids, with improvements over 20%. This technique, which uses suitable percentage of Fe dopant in Au shells, demonstrates a cutting-edge approach for harvesting solar energy flux collection. Meanwhile, an optimal enhancement in
A m
of DASC was demonstrated with Au ellipsoids (Els) up to 54% and Ag NEs over 86%. Furthermore, the findings demonstrated that NF-based direct collectors could obtain
A m
closer to the ideal solar radiation absorber condition by using Au NPs, setting low volume fraction, as well as ensuring a high particle surface area (AR = 4) in NFs, integrated with solar collector thickness of 1 cm.

These outcomes present that optimized NP compositions, morphologies and sizes hold huge promises for enhancing performances of DASC, and exploring them closer to the ideal absorber state. By tuning the specific characteristics of plasmonic nanomaterials, we lead for more effective and durable solar energy harvesting, opening a significant avenue in the search for clean and cost-effective energy resources by exploring novel and innovative techniques.

Particles properties including shape, morphology, composition, and surface chemistry affect the characteristics of NFs. In order to effectively assess thermophysical qualities, these properties could be incorporated into hybrid models. To create accurate predictive frameworks, the researchers have used a variety of methods, such as radial basis function neural networks (RBF-NN), adaptive network-based fuzzy inference systems (ANFIS), and hybrid Group Method of Data Handling Polynomial Neural Networks (GMDH-PNN) models [341–343]. To properly evaluate thermal conductivity, these models consider several factors, like temperature, volume fraction, base-fluid characteristics, and NP size. Experimental evaluations and numerical simulations have validated these hybrid models enhanced prediction performance. For scientists and engineers working in a variety of fields that depend on NFs for heat-transfer applications, these modeling advanced techniques provide insightful information.

An effort to improve the efficiency, hybrid models have been developed in response to the derive to increase NF thermal conductivity. For example, Azari et al. [341] presented a GMDH-PNN model to forecast NF thermal conductivity. Six hidden layers with two inputs each made up this model, which included variables including NP size, volume fraction, and base-fluid properties. With RMSE of 0.03027 for the training dataset and 0.02843 for validation, the model demonstrated remarkable accuracy through training using least squares regression. The efficiency of hybrid GMDH-PNN model in improving thermal conductivity prediction across different NFs was confirmed by a comparative analysis with current models, which showed the model higher prediction performance. Meanwhile, Barati-Harooni et al. [342] introduced RBF-NN model that provides accurate viscosity estimation based on 1490 experimental data points. With an overall R-squared value of 0.99996 and low errors (AARD% = 0.2, RMSE = 0.0089), the model shows great accuracy. Superior performance above current models and correlations were found through comparative research. Sensitivity study showed that temperature had the least effect on viscosity, but NP volume fraction had the greatest influence. Further, Baghban et al. [343] discussed difficulties in predicting NF viscosity, which is significant for heat-transfer applications. They explored ANFIS model by collecting 1277 experimental data-points. ANFIS predicted NF relative viscosity (NF-RV) with little variance and an exceptional R-squared of 0.99997. According to comparative study, ANFIS outperformed other models and produced NF-RV predictions that were remarkably accurate. This model enables reliable viscosity projections, leading remarkable for engineers and chemists engaged in NF-based heat transfer applications.

A semi-analytical technique is used to investigate the laminar boundary layer flow of TiO
2
-CuO hybrid NF over stationary/moving wedges or corners, also referred to as the Falkner-Skan issue. With potential factors like Prandtl number, form factor, Falkner-Skan power law, and wedge-corner movement, the authors developed a novel mass-based hybrid NF model considering base-fluid and NP masses [344]. Through the evaluation of numerical solutions and the transformation of leading equations, they were able to obtain exact agreement with the body of existing literature. They found that increasing the Falkner-Skan power law parameter and the second NP mass reduced the widths of the thermal and hydrodynamic boundary layers. Additionally, as compared to its base-fluid counterpart, a hybrid model demonstrated ideal thermal efficiency. Kotha et al. [345] introduced a novel hybrid model, numerically indicating improved flow characteristics at nodal/saddle stagnation points. Their findings incorporated radiative heat transfer as well as magnetic fields using the Tiwari-Das NF tools, and novel direct mathematical calculations. Further, seven hundred and fifteen experimental data points from several hybrid NP research were employed for constructing support vector regression (SVR) and multilayer perceptron ANN models. The performance validation of the model utilizes R
2
and MSE. During the validation phase, the ANN algorithm presented R
2
value of 0.99997, while the SVR model demonstrates an R
2
-value of 0.99788. These metrics indicate the high accuracy and reliability of both models in predicting the behavior of hybrid NFs [273]. A unique hybrid model developed by Kotha et al., [345], which quantitatively indicated enhanced flow characteristics at nodal/saddle stagnation locations. Their results included new direct mathematical computations, magnetic fields, and radiative heat transfer using the Tiwari-Das NF tools. Additionally, 715 experimental data points from various hybrid NP studies were used to build multilayer perceptron ANN and support vector regression (SVR) models. The MSE and R
2
were used to validate the model performance. The ANN model displayed a R
2
value of 0.99997 during the validation phase, whereas the SVR model displayed a R
2
value of 0.99788. These measurements demonstrate how well both models anticipate the behavior of hybrid NFs with high accuracy and dependability [273].

Another study explored the relationship between NP volumetric fractions and comparable components, whether the results of the homogeneous model for NFs matched solutions from regular problems. By adjusting these factors, the study improved knowledge of mixed convection processes in rotating disk systems and offered insightful information about how hybrid NF behaves when employing a generalized hybrid model. Their prospective applications in a variety of fluid dynamic scenarios were made easier by this greater knowledge, highlighting the need of studying hybrid NF behavior in broad contexts [346,347]. Table 7 shows the distinct effects of hybrid models on their performance.

There are still significant gaps and limitations in the field of hybrid NFs related to the development and use of hybrid models. While some studies focus on mixed convection phenomena and boundary layer flow assessments, others emphasize improving thermal conductivity forecasts with cutting-edge techniques like GMDH-PNN and ANFIS models. However, there are differences in how these models are formulated, especially when it comes to integrating various NP features, base-fluid properties, and environmental conditions. These variations demonstrate the need for established procedures to guarantee accuracy and consistency across applications of hybrid models. Furthermore, there are still ongoing differences in the methods used for validation and comparison, which results in different degrees of accuracy and dependability in different research projects. Addressing these gaps and differences becomes crucial to improving our understanding and application of hybrid models for consistently and accurately predicting fluid dynamic behaviors and thermal conductivity across various environments.

To develop single hybrid model, the several investigations are actively addressing current gaps in the effort to develop and employ hybrid models. To build an in-depth framework that can accurately evaluate the characteristics and dynamics of hybrid NFs in a variety of situations and applications. These techniques use several approaches, involving theoretical analyses, experimental validations, and numerical simulations [273]. The primary objective is to create a standardized model that enables reliability as well as consistency in predicting fluid dynamics and thermal conductivity, unifying the multifaceted techniques and parameters utilized in current models. It is noteworthy that Table 8
presents several operating parameters that can affect the efficacy of system employing hybrid model. One can see that the thermal conductivity of hybrid NFs is evaluated using seven distinct input variables namely, temperature, volume concentration, the base-fluid, NPs bulk density, external factors, the thermal conductivity, and particle type. Hence, a universal formula for predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid NFs is required.

To assess a crucial impact of thermal conductivity due to hybrid NF, a study shows the changes in temperature distribution and heat transfer rate, highlighting the crucial significance of hybrid mixed convection. Furthermore, temperature, Nusselt number, and skin friction along the stretched surface are all greatly impacted by variations in the NPs volume fraction and mixed convection parameters. In particular, the average Nusselt values of the various models of thermal conductivity clearly differ from one another, with the Hamilton-Crosser model producing the lowest averages, followed by the Yamada-Ota and Xue models [348].

Considering gaps in validation methods, model formulations, and input operating parameters necessitate to develop a unified hybrid model, requiring broad comparative studies and robust testing. Several studies propose developing a single framework that best predicts the behavior of hybrid NFs through carefully analyzing and combining numerous methods. Thereby, it is imperative to enhance interdisciplinary cooperation and knowledge exchange between experts from diverse disciplines, such as materials science, nanotechnology, and fluid dynamics. Through gathering knowledge and experience from other disciplines, a completer and more reliable hybrid model can be designed. This model can facilitate in advanced areas including energy storage, heat transfer applications, and thermal management.

The potential of NFs to enhance heat-transfer and energy performance in numerous industries (see Fig. 16 ), particularly the automotive sector, has garnered significant attention [349]. Researchers have recently shown how NFs can be used in a variety of automotive applications, from fuel additives and engine lubricants to radiator coolants [350]. Coolant testing using NFs as a replacement is currently undertaken in the automotive industry. Research is focused on the use of NFs as radiator coolants since they provide better heat transfer performance than traditional fluids [351]. The radiator, which normally uses water as a heat-transfer medium, is essential in removing extra heat from the engine. As seen in Fig. 17 , coolant constantly flows through the engine block channels, absorbing heat and transferring it to other parts. Numerous studies show that using NFs, including both metallic (such as Cu and Al) and non-metallic (such as multiwalled carbon nanotube) varieties, significantly improves the total heat transfer coefficient in automobile radiators [352]. When using nano-coolants (NFs as a coolant) instead of de-ionized water at the same Reynolds number, the overall heat-transfer coefficient clearly increases. Significant improvements in the overall heat-transfer coefficient were observed, with the highest improvements over 40% for MWCNT, 29% for Cu, and 25% for Al based NFs. Additionally, the same pumping power requirement was used to evaluate the coolants’ thermal performance. Furthermore, Hafeez and Co. showed remarkable outcomes by adding NPs such TiO
2
, CuO, and Al
2
O
3
, which enhanced coolant thermal conductivity and ultimately improved heat-dissipation efficiency. Their results demonstrate the role that thermal slip effects play in lowering fluid temperature. CuO NF presented the least effect, but TiO
2
NF showed the most thermal performance. Al
2
O
3
NF has the best thermal performance out of the three NF that were examined. Additionally, the impact of coolant inlet temperature on the Nusselt number presented by Sandhya and colleagues [353]. Using a TiO
2
NF combined with H
2
O and EG in varying ratios, they discovered that the 60:40% water-ethylene glycol mixture produced superior outcomes. They also considered variables including inlet coolant temperature, flow rate, and particle loading in their experimental investigation. According to their findings, the Nusselt number was significantly enhanced by coolant flow rate and NPs suspension, although only a minor improvement enhanced inlet temperature.

With their improved tribological qualities and decreased friction, NFs show great promise as engine lubricants as well as radiator coolants. Using chemically treated CNTs distributed in EG as lubricants has been shown to increase dynamic viscosity and thermal conductivity [354]. Furthermore, hybrid NFs, which combine many NPs in a mixture, have become cutting-edge heat transfer fluids with better heat transfer coefficients and thermal conductivity than traditional fluids [91]. According to Mathew et al. [355], between 10 to 15% of the fuel energy is considered for friction between engine moving parts. Due to the relative motion between moving parts, this friction creates unwanted forces that reduce engine performance. The traditional usage of fluids including lubricants, air, and water for cooling in a variety of applications, such as industrial, automotive, and locomotive systems, was presented in another study [356]. In their experimental analysis, they discussed on lubricating property, focusing on investigating the thermal-related conductivity of working fluids containing coal-ash, silica and Al
2
O
3
NPs. Their findings also demonstrated the efficiency of novel biological substance by adding a lubricant as compared to traditional fluids. NFs with multiple volume concentrations (0.2%, 0.4%, 0.6%, and 1.2%) were made with SAEOW-20 engine oil as the basis fluid. These tests showed that NFs have a significant potential to increase lubrication performance and thermal conductivity in automotive systems.

For millennia, mankind inspired by space –the wide range beyond Earth environment that inspires inquisitiveness, leads exploration as well as fuels innovation. In particular, space governs as a unlimited canvas of amazement, driving investigation into the hidden secrets of black hole and capturing the fascination of distant galaxies. Through modern spacecrafts and telescopes, scientists and researchers have unraveled the layers of heavenly mysteries, exploring the creation of humanity and the universe. Meanwhile, the space investigation has not only broadened our vision of cosmos but has also stimulated technological progress for the benefits of human life on this planet, covering from medical research to satellite communication to conduct in micro-gravity territory [357,358]. In habitat, the military equipment and space stations produce high heat fluxes, the traditional fluids struggle to generate efficient cooling. Thereby, to cope such task, state-of-the-art thermal fluids integrated with heat-sinks appropriately reduce excessive heat into the environment or recycle it into further applications. It is examined that directed energy weapons can create heat fluxes over 1000 W/cm
2
, while military equipment including submarines, cruise ships, and vehicles also face significant heat flux issues [359]. Hence, the researchers have demonstrated their potentials to effectively remove excessive heat by exploring the robust thermal properties of NFs, containing metallic NPs suspensions. The findings demonstrated that particles colloidal surpass base-fluids during critical heat flux conditions, particularly in pool boiling scenarios.

In a plethora of studies evaluating the integration of NFs in thermal systems, as presented in Fig. 18 , the researchers demonstrated the performance and efficiency enhancements obtained through various approaches.

Flat plate solar collectors (FPSC) and other simple solar thermal systems operate at relatively low temperatures between 100 and 150
°
C [360,361]. Through integration of turbulators and NFs into FPSC demonstrated to possess an impact and provided understanding into how these components influence thermal efficiency. Numerous studies determined to explore important parameters such as NP type, base-fluid, volume percentage, and overall system efficiency by analyzing thermal efficiency. Al
2
O
3
, CuO, and TiO 2 are frequently used NFs, with volume-fractions ranging from 0 to 2% [360]. A very popular turbulator that helps achieve thermal efficiency over 86.5%, is twisted tape. Additionally, a possible maximum efficiency of about 84.85% has been obtained when turbulators and NFs are used together, indicating the possibility of synergistic advantages. According to research outcomes, introducing 0.5% Al
2
O
3
as well as 0.5% crystal nano-cellulose (CNC) based mixtures to FPSC enhanced performance over 2.48% and 8.46%, respectively [362]. Meanwhile, Hawwash and Co. [363] demonstrated that presence of particles in base-fluid of FPSC governed to enhancement in pressure drop, ultimately thermal performance. Furthermore, the ideal configuration is achieved when the volume-fraction of CuO NPs is placed at 0.5%, which yields the best collector thermal efficiency.

To explore the performance of thermal collectors, energy and exergy (EnE) are treated to be crucial parameters. Using thorough evaluations, single and hybrid NFs both significantly enhance FPSC efficiency by handling the issues, such as viscosity and instability, recognizing pivotal parameters that can influence efficacy. Achieving significant increase in energy and efficiency can be obtained through integrating porous media with NFs, illustrating a practical approach for advanced solar collectors. These findings showed the importance of NFs in the latest techniques to transform solar thermal technology to open new horizons for sustainable and effective energy systems [364]. In experimental contexts, the use of NFs in FPSC has produced encouraging outcomes. The EnE efficiencies of FPSC using four different types of NFs such as MgO/water, Al
2
O
3
/water, TiO
2
/water, and CuO/water, were assessed and contrasted with traditional based fluid [365]. The research included NFs with water and nanomaterial volume fractions of 1-4% and flow-rate between 1 and 4 L/min. CuO/water NF showed the greatest EnE efficiency values for the solar collector among the NFs that were studied. In particular, the CuO NFs-operated solar collector had EnE efficiencies of 38.21% and 34.06%, respectively. In particular, functionalized carbon nanoplatelets and water-based NFs augmented with metal oxide NPs showed better thermal conductivities. Significant improvements in thermal efficiency, ranging from 12.36% to 17.45%, were seen when FPSCs using these NFs were compared to those using distilled water [366].

Together, these research outcomes provide in-depth insights into the advantages and applications of NFs, presenting remarkable perspectives to maximize solar thermal systems. Notably, the exergy efficiency of the thermal collectors serve as an appropriate index for evaluating the overall performance of the collector systems, given as [367]:
(37)

Q useful
