,

As shown Fig. 18.b, the DASC is frequently used alongside with concentrators and is essential in thermal collector applications. The working fluid in this configuration circulates inside a clear (glass) receiver. When concentrated solar energy reaches the receiver, the working fluid quickly absorbs it. Instead of great potential of using NFs in these systems, the implication of water as the base-fluid is attributed to low efficiency. Zheng et al., [368] demonstrated optothermal energy conversion traits of mono-MWCNT and hybrid-MWCNT-TiN NFs for DASCs, presenting outstanding optical absorption capacity. Their findings further revealed that TiN-based NFs greatly enhanced absorption efficiency, providing optimal gains over 6.9% due to LPR effects. Through tuning the optical characteristics for DASC, Farooq et al. [16] developed a novel strategy to increase energy harvesting. The use of doped NS (Fe-Au) in working fluid for DASC is the focal point. Their results revealed that, under ideal circumstances, the introduction of doped NS provided effective increase in the
A m
factor, possibly outperforming thermal efficiencies of 95%. These outcomes represent a significant advancement over traditional Au NS-based working fluids. Similarly, metal nitirdes nanospheres distributed in aqueous medium were investigated for thermo-optical performance for DASC by Vital et al. [46]. According to their research, these transition metal nitride NPs had strong absorption peaks and low scattering cross-sections, which aligned well with sun spectrum. Collectors using all three nitrides NPs showed significant increases in thermal efficiency over 50%, 36% and 41%, respectively, as compared to Au NF, a reference NF. The use of ligand-free TiN NFs in acetone as NFs, created by PLAL, was investigated by the same group. According to their research, these NPs have a minimal size dispersion (mean size of 25 nm) with broad absorbance peak at about 700 nm. Additionally, their -Ve charge allowed solutions to remain electrostatically stable for an extended period of time. Further, under 1.25-sun illumination, the NF temperatures rise to 29
°
C, and the
A m
of TiN NFs approaches 95.7% at relatively low volume fractions. According to their results, DASCs that used TiN NF showed over 80% higher thermal efficiency than those of Au NPs based NFs.

The addition of NFs to compound parabolic collectors (CPCs) has been the subject of several studies, including Fe
3
O
4
and Al
2
O
3
. These investigations have produced significant improvements in thermal efficiency [369]. Researchers have clarified fluid flow dynamics by using numerical calculations with ANSYS software, attaining flow rates of up to 9.3 mL/s. Experiments carried out in real-world climate circumstances have demonstrated flow rates as high as 10.78 mL/s, which supports these findings. The collector thermal performance of metallic as well as non-metallic oxide NFs, such as MWCNT-H
2
O and Al
2
O
3
-H
2
O, has also been shown to increase significantly, outperforming conventional fluids by as much as 33% [370].

Using computer models, the improved heat transfer has been achieved by integration of internal barriers in PTSCs, especially with Cu-based NFs providing greater Nusselt numbers [371]. It was found that SWCNT-EO NF remarkably more effective, attaining efficiency gains ranging from 1.6% to 14.9%, when compared to base-fluids [372]. Further, the researchers achieved the best configurations for optimizing thermal performance via computer simulations to investigate the impacts of oval rib parameters on TiO
2
/water NF flow in PTSCs [ [250]. Overall, these findings underscored the great potential of NFs in advancing thermal technology.

The prior outcomes, overall, present the significance how thermal collectors using solar energy possess a bright future in exploring renewable and cost-effective energy sources. Notably, the individual project contributes significantly to improving the impact and efficiency of solar thermal systems, whether it is developing NF-based technology or advancing absorber designs. Researchers are deriving significant strides in energy generation and use by utilizing cutting-edge techniques including internal fins and the combination of metallic and non-metallic NPs, in addition to careful absorber design. The future of solar thermal collectors seems brighter as research into these fields continues, signaling a dramatic change to sustainable and environmental friendly power solutions.

To achieve durable desalination techniques, novel methods blending nanotechnology integrated with solar power have emerged. In a study demonstrated by Rafiei and Co. [373], desalination system using solar illumination was meticulously investigated, coupling with humidification dehumidification desalination as well as PVT panels, and a focal point concentrator serving as heat source. Their analysis searched for the influence of various NFs, including CuO, TiO
2
, MWCNT, and Al
2
O
3
into the base-fluid (oil), employed as solar working fluids. Further, their investigations demonstrated that enhancing the particles concentration could improve both cavity heat gain and thermal efficiency, while Cu-based NFs exhibiting exceptional performance. However, dense concentrations led to decrease thermal efficiency, underscoring a suitable balance between freshwater generation and system performance. Further, it aligns with Singh and Co. [374] research that highlights the potential of NFs in improving desalination system performance. Additionally, Meng et al. [375] developed cutting-edge technique using tungsten carbide (WC) NFs to enhance the efficiency for desalination and wastewater treatment through solar evaporation. Their findings demonstrated that WC-based NFs containing 0.3 wt% NPs absorbed 99% of solar illumination. Moreover, the optical thermal conversion performance achieved is up to 97%. This state-of-the-art device showed evaporation rate of 1.235 kg m
− 2
h
− 1
, thus attaining evaporation efficiency over 74%. Similarly, the induction of NFs into solar-based desalination mirrors, the comprehensive goal of sustainable desalination systems pinpointed by Singh et al. [376], prioritizing decreasing energy consumption and reducing environmental effects.

Furthermore, the use of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs)-based NFs for desalination systems exhibits a remarkable development in solving the stability related concerns associated with carbon NFs [377]. By utilization of moderate and average manufacturing process, CQDs with higher stability can be developed and comprehensively evaluated. These QDs-based NFs presented durability in saline water across different NaCl concentrations as well as environmental conditions. Furthermore, their outstanding optical characteristics and thermal stability highlighted the potential to use them for solar desalination utilization. The use of QDs-based NFs paved the way to achieve maximum improvements in water production and energy efficiency than that of conventional solar stills, see Fig. 19 . Based on economic analysis, QDs indicate promising water production costs. Additionally, Zhang and Co. [377] presented that in the traditional solar still, the cumulative water productivity was 521 mL, while in the solar still containing QDs-based NFs, it improved to 823 mL, highlighting a significant enhancement in water production of 57.9%. Meanwhile, the study conducted by Elango et al. [378] also demonstrated the impacts of NFs-based still in elevating water production, specifically with Al
2
O
3
NFs. Further, the investigations derived analytical expression for characteristic equation, thereby assuming three distinct NFs at optimized concentration of metallic particles (0.25%) [177]. The findings showed significantly higher thermal exergy and efficiency for NFs (TiO
2
46.10%; Al
2
O
3
50.34%; CuO 43.81%) in comparison to the base-fluids (37.78% and 4.92%, respectively). Therefore, the yield under several weather operating conditions was evaluated in March using the proposed model, as illustrated in Fig. 20 .

Nanofluids are crucial, specifically in medical sector, and studies have shown their valuable contribution in several fields. The key applications include, such as biological component chilling, photodynamic therapy (PPD), cryopreservation, wound dressing, and photothermal therapy (PTT) disease diagnosis, drug administration, and antimicrobial applications, as shown in Fig. 21 . Nevertheless, a comprehensive analysis of plasmonic NFs traits–such as their characteristics, uniform distribution, morphology, size, biocompatibility, and crystallinity–necessitate to guarantee their quick and safe use in medical sectors. Considering wide range of applications, the working fluids have obtained much attention recently. To increase the heat transfer performance, rigorous investigations underscore the importance of stable NPs within base-fluids, such as EG, water, and oil [379]. These investigations made possible using several approaches ascribed in the literature to present potential applications in a variety of fields, like transportation, and medicine. To utilize in medical applications, Sheikhpour and Co. [380] developed NFs, highlighting their invaluable contribution in imaging, medication administration, and the fight against antibiotic resistance. To address current demands in advance medical procedures, efficient antibacterial treatments and targeted drug administration are now achieved because of NFs ability to diffuse uniformly and operate in stable conditions.

Currently, plasmonic working fluids are employed in optical sensors for disease detection [36]. The investigation notably identifies the dynamic and nonlinear optical characteristics of Au particles in biomedical domain, presenting their enhanced sensitivity and encouraging potential for diagnostics in nanomedicine [381]. For example, the optimization of a novel label-free solution-based LPR immunosensor developed by Farooq et al., and they concentrated on possible parameters such molecular sensitivity for Dengue virus. They determined the ideal size of spherical-shaped Au NPs and showed how well they work to achieve high performance metrics, such as a sharp spectral peak and improved molecular sensitivity, by utilizing 3D full-wave field analysis and computer simulations. According to their results, the sensor can detect dengue NS1 antigen concentrations up to 1.50 nM.

Magnetic Fluid Hyperthermia (MFH) uses alternating magnetic fields to heat cancer cells using magnetic NFs. The focus has switched to 100–200 nm nano-carriers for multimodal MFH that use enhanced penetration and retention (EPR) in malignant tissues in conjunction with medication delivery or PDT. The study explored radio-frequency alternating magnetic field-induced heating in magnetically polarizable oil-in-water nanoemulsions with a hydrodynamic diameter of approximately 200 nm, containing single domain superparamagnetic NPs with a diameter of approximately 10 nm in the oil phase. It also demonstrated the impact of medium viscosity and droplet size polydispersity on heating efficiency at 164.4 +/- 4.3 W/gFe, the specific absorption rate (SAR) peaks [382]. In a different work, the Ribeiro et al. investigated the possibility of using Ag NPs based LPR phenomenon to enhance riboflavin-mediated Photodynamic Inactivation (PDI) of microorganisms. On Streptococcus mutans, however, their findings indicated that LPR of pectin-coated Ag NPs could enhance the photodynamic action [383].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactor-heat exchanger integration: improving performance and efficacy
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 22 , using advanced NFs, the optimized heat transfer in nuclear reactors can be achieved by addressing miniaturization challenges. It is noteworthy that enhanced heat transfer in compact nuclear reactors by incorporating NFs provided promising solutions for effective power generation in multifunctional systems. Currently, the research has demonstrated the potential impact of NFs to significantly improve the safety as well as performance of nuclear systems. When NPs suspended into base-fluid, they exhibited outstanding heat-transfer features as compared to conventional coolants like water [384]. The findings further indicated that the heat-transfer efficiency of Al
2
O
3
-based NFs improved with enhancing concentrations, while the volume-fraction phi remains in the range of 2.0%. Nevertheless, this tendency reversed as the volume-fraction of particles exceeded up to 2%. Conversely, in comparison to traditional coolant (water), Al
2
O
3
-based NFs with 2% volume-fraction demonstrated the most remarkable heat transfer, reaching over 103%. Consequently, using Al
2
O
3
NFs as a coolant could effectively increase heat transfer, thus improving safety limits. Thereby, the increase in heat transport is critical for improving the thermal-hydraulic features of nuclear reactor cores, potentially reducing the over-heating effects.

Meanwhile, in another study [385], it is demonstrated that hybrid NFs presented huge potential to evaluate the heat transfer characteristics for VVER-1000 nuclear rectors. These investigations compared the effectiveness of hybrid NFs with water as coolants, when the volume-fraction of hybrid NPs, such as Cu, CuO, and TiO
2
mixed with 0.1% Al
2
O
3
in the coolant increased, as a result the thermal conductivity decreased. Additionally, in pure water coolant, the maximum temperature reached was 612.68 K at the end of the channel. Although, the remarkable temperature variation, measuring 617.35 K among unique particles, was examined in case of hybrid NFs (0.1% of Al
2
O
3
+ 0.1% of TiO
2
). The investigation showed that increasing the volume-proportion of hybrid colloids in sub-channel geometry could improve heat transfer efficiency. Employing hybrid NFs within safe operating limits can also ensure batter safety in reactor operation through minimum DNBR values.

Besides such advantages, various limitations and challenges related to stability as well as uniform dispersion of NPs in base-fluids persist over time. Hence, this highlights the significance of advanced research to cope these issues with cutting-edge techniques and utilize potentiality in nuclear systems, including radiation shielding as well as in-vessel corium retention strategies during severe accidents [386]. Considering everything in detail, the utilization of NFs in nuclear systems provide a favorable avenue to improve the performance, safety, and suitability for next-generation reactor technologies.

Over past two decades, with advanced technology, the utilization of NFs in upgrading heat transfer has increased, specifically in areas, such as building technology, renewable energy systems, cooling technology. Therefore, the remarkable advancements have been observed in this field, including the development of models for predicting features and heat-transfer characteristics, the identification of enhancement mechanisms, experimental measurements of heat-related properties, and exploration of numerous applications of NFs for heat-transfer improvement. The scientists have searched for several domains including NFs in cooling technology and making cavities to contain NFs for enhanced heat transfer rates. Therefore, they investigated numerous cavity shapes and designs, each providing unique merits in utilization in the range of electronic cooling systems to nuclear reactors.

NFs provide promising potentials for free cooling utilization in building technology. Recently, the novel nano phase change materials (e.g. Paraffin RT21) have been developed by inducting NPs, such as MWCNT into phase change materials (PCM) [387]. These PCM manipulated thermal properties for storing and releasing thermal energy more efficiently. As a result, PCM can be employed in buildings for free cooling purposes, minimizing reliance on traditional air-conditioning systems. In addition, in another study, Zeng et al. [388] have concentrated on investigating the application of NFs (Al
2
O
3
/H
2
O) in microchannel heat exchangers with the goal of improving their thermal efficiency even more. Combining NFs with modern flow disruption patterns, e.g. micro-channels with rectangular grooves, has depicted remarkable potential in improving heat transfer efficiency. The results further demonstrate that Nusselt number of the Rectangular Grooved Microchannel (RGMC) containing NFs (2 vol%) outperforms as compared to smooth micro-channel (SMC) with the same NFs by 38.5%. Further, it improves the Nu of RGMC with pure H
2
O by 36.7%. These findings illustrated the effective improvement in heat-transfer performance of the innovatively designed RGMC structures. Due to interaction between NFs and flow structures, better thermal efficiency achieved, which indicates potentials for the construction of highly effective micro-channel heat exchangers for building air-conditioning systems.

Furthermore, tailoring hybrid NFs to achieve precise and controlled heat transfer properties is crucial to meet several applications including cooling systems and automotive technologies. Yaseen et al. [389] performed computational modeling to provide a numerical solution for wall jet flow over a stretching surface, aiming on a non-Newtonian Eyring-Powell hybrid NF comprising CoFe
2
O
4
, TiO
2
, and water. Considering the impact of magnetic field, waste output concentration, and irregular heat sources contribute novelty to the research. The results demonstrate that optimal heat source parameters remarkably enhance fluid temperature. Conversely, the enhanced values of external and local pollutant source parameters decrease mass transfer rate. These results are applicable to a wide range of wall jet applications, including as car defrosters, sluice gate flows, spray paint drying processes for cars or buildings, laptop CPU cooling systems, and cooling jets for turbo-machinery components. To solve dimensionless differential equations of the system, Afsar et al. [390] employed the built-in NDSolve command in the computational package Mathematica. The results of their research confirm that the incorporation of NPs significantly improves heat-transfer processes. Specifically, an elevation in Joule heating and electromagnetic parameters leads to a heightened heat-transfer rate at boundary. Furthermore, Al
2
O
3
bar charts have been employed to present variations in the crucial parameter known as the average heat transfer rate. In a scenario of constant heating, the optimal choice for enhancing the system heat transfer rate is the utilization of Al
2
O
3
NFs.

The extensive research and development on NFs indicate that these nanomaterials are becoming more and more important for improving heat-transfer in variety of technological applications. Along with developments in material science and nanotechnology, more research into NF-based solutions shows promise for enhancing performance and efficiency in building, renewable energy, and cooling technologies.

NFs have massive potential for applications in several fields and were considered very useful in corrosion prevention in aerospace, automotive, and packaging applications. For instance, Gao et al. [391] illustrated that the addition of nanoparticles reduces the corrosion current density of 316 L stainless steel under quaternary mixed molten salt conditions. Specifically, the corrosion current density in molten salt NFs containing SiO
2
and TiO
2
was reduced to 0.636 mA /
cm 2
and 0.749 mA /
cm 2
, respectively. Notably, NF with 0.05% by weight of ZnO NPs exhibited the highest corrosion prevention efficiency among those tested. The stability period of these NFs exceeded 36 months [392].

Moreover, adding 6 wt% NFs at 25
°
C increased the convective heat transfer coefficient by over 36.1%. These NFs improved heat transfer and provided effective corrosion protection and impact resistance [393]. The introduction of ZnO resulted in a maximum corrosion mass gain and rate for austenitic stainless steel in the NFs of 1.72 mg /
cm 2
and 9.6 μ m/year, respectively, which corresponded to reductions of 27.4% and 25.8% compared to the base salt [394]. Further, doping of NPs in molten salts demonstrated a major decrease in the corrosion rate by about 50% as compared to the base salt [395]. After introducing NPs into the oxide scale, it came out to be a NPs reticulated
Al 2
O 3
with a much more homogeneous and denser oxide layer. Thus improving the hardness of the oxide by a factor of about 30% due to the increase in thickness of the protective barrier capability. Hybrid MXene/C-dot NFs demonstrated excellent thermal stability, mitigated corrosion effects, and significantly enhanced photothermal conversion efficiency [396].

In this section, the preparation and discussion of effervescent tablets for thermal applications are presented. MWCNT NFs were prepared by the addition of MWCNTs to SDS surfactant and effervescing agents. Indeed, the obtained results show that the increment of effervescent agents enhances the CO
2
bubble generation from the tablets, thereby increasing the dispersion stability of the suspension [397]. This is found to prolong the preparation time of NFs. Moreover, it is noted that the incorporation of the powder formulation of tablets through ball milling exhibits residuals that significantly influence the mechanism of NFs development. Physically, the NFs prepared from effervescent tablets were more stable, had enhanced thermal conductivity, and were cost-effective unlike traditional ways of dispersions. A separate study explored the crystallization processes within NFs produced from carbon-based effervescent tablets that included MWCNTs and additional substances [398]. Accordingly, it was obtained that NFs in distilled water had higher thermal conductivity and stability than those in SW. In addition, the crystallization rate in distilled water was obtained to increase with the increase in the higher concentration of MWCNTs subjected to the NFs. Such tablet-based NFs present an alternative production methodology with increased stability and efficient thermal conductivity at low cost compared to conventional methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges, outlook, environmental and health effects
ce:section.ce:para: Undoubtedly metallic NPs are considered as a prominent class of materials with superb thermal transport properties, but still these materials undergoing immense research which unveiling their several facets. So, prior to synthesize plasmonic NFs for critical applications, there are several concerns that needed to be tackle first like health care, distinct composites of NPs and their process, stability and modes of heat conductivity which must be entirely reconsidered (Fig. 23 ). Till now many experimental results of NPs are not as good as what were expected, additionally, theoretically estimated results also not match with the experimental results. Many published works reveal that the idea of liquid layering at solid-liquid boundary is not adequate to state the thermal conductivity strengthening of plasmonic NFs. The net outcome of different processes like liquid layering, particular surface area of the enormous sized NPs, and the Brownian movement of NPs are possibly the most influencing factors to observe the exceptional increase in plasmonic nanofluidic thermal conductivity. Plasmonic NFs thermal conductivity is decreased if thermal conductivity of the metallic particle is reduced. There will be aggregation of particles if their chemical bond strength changes. In such case the introduction of surface charge as a surfactant, to repel particles, would be helpful to avoid their aggregation. In terms of particle synthesis and symmetry, the key challenges in plasmonic NPs application of NFs are the control on particle shape, size and active functionalization. Another challenge is to maintain the NFs collector efficiency which is hampered by its increased temperature and significant heat loss. The collector caused deficiency can be impeded by creating a better collector design. In another approach produced heat loss can also be avoided by the introduction of transparent thermal insulating material which in turn stop the heat convection from the top surface of the material. Another vital parameter of a plasmonic NFs is absorption coefficient, which is directly related to DASC optothermal efficiency. Absorption coefficient of the NFs will increase if the value of spectral absorption coefficient is high. The absorption and scattering phenomenon are particle size dependent. If the particles are smaller in size, then absorption phenomenon will dominate compared to the scattering. Therefore, precise estimation of the absorption as well as scattering coefficients of plasmonic-based NF is compulsory.

It is an established fact that LSPR induced absorption peak is profoundly affected by the shape and size of NPs [399]. By tuning the size and changing the shape of the NPs, photothermal conversion effectiveness of the plasmonic NF can be improved by manifolds. It has been reported that NF encompassing range of different sizes NPs leads to the broadband absorption behavior compared to the single size containing NPs-based fluid [400]. Due to the occurrence of broad sun radiation spectrum and a slender absorption spectral peak in visible region of metallic NPs, plasmonic NF with metal spherical particles exhibits unsatisfactory performance in near-IR range. In order to get the up to the mark broad band absorption, it is suggested to synthesize hybrid core-shell or mixed noble metal particles [86]. In another report, hybrid plasmonic NF solution encompassing combination of nanoellipsoids, nanorod and nanosheet was prepared to obtain the broadband absorption in visible and near-IR regions [86]. Besides the good performance of noble metal particles, high cost is a big hindrance in their extensive utilization. Comparatively, low-cost hybrid particles like TiN show LSPR feature resembling to the high-cost noble metals but at much lower cost. It is further reported that TiN NPs are contemplated as exceptional material to substitute noble metals in photothermal conversion applications due to its superb optical and photothermal adoption efficacy [401].

The practical implications of plasmonic NFs in outdoor required it to be enough robust to endure the severe working conditions as expected in solar volumetric-absorption methods like thermal sequencing, higher temperature, and fluid shear. Under these circumstances destabilization elements can compromise the NFs colloidal and chemical stability and ultimately decreasing its operational functioning and life cycle performance. Clustering, sedimentation that leads to oxidation and dissolution are the generally occurring problems. Although its interaction with the solar radiation has been determined as utmost stimulating destabiliser but it still stands as a least studied topic. In general manifestation of excellent optical characteristics of the plasmonic nanofluids are impeded to as-synthesize stipulation with no concern to robustness of these features on exposure to the solar spectrum throughout outdoor operations [402]. Among several available NPs and their hybrids like CNT- Al
2
O
3
, Cu-TiO
2
, Al
2
O
3
-MWCNT, CNT-Cu, etc., the appropriate choice of plasmonic NPs and their synthesis process play a key role in determining a unique application due to their distinct features. Thereby, before choosing a particular NP, in-depth study of its behavior is required. The synthesis procedure reported in the mentioned work is a bit complex, high cost and time consuming as compared to the regular NF synthesis process. Its prime advantages encompass extraordinary intensity and flexibility of the LSPR. Despite the fact, ideal or 100 % photo-thermal efficiency can not be achieved due to the scattering effects. It also doesn’t depend on the volume of metal NPs. So far, different methodologies have been employed including modification of the plasmonic NPs shape to minimize the scattering element. Mallah and co-workers [403] have revealed that NPs of Ag possess substantially low scattering in the range of LSPR wavelengths. In the published literature, there are some other challenges liked with the plasmonic NFs like synthesis procedure, its cost and stability issues which have become constraints in their realistic applications [404,405]. The typical synthesis procedure comprises of ion-exchange chemical reactions and the subsequent solutions accompanied with the residual ions and leave undesirable effects in functional applications. The issue of lacking in long lasting stability due to aggregation and weak Van der Waals forces, their thermal, optical and rheological attributes are severely compromised. Further, high material cost of noble metals also restricts their exploitation at commercial scale. Elevated viscosity of NF is also determined as one of its negative aspect due to high pumping-power requirement. The specific heat of NF is reduced by dispersing the NP in the mainstream fluid.

With passage of time and ample research efforts, researchers around the world have developed various techniques to enhance the conventional NFs stability. But plasmonic NF stability till date remain as a hefty challenge. One of the reasons behind this is the distinct charge densities at the surface of different NPs. But altogether, surfactant type, NP size and base-fluid are some of the factors that influence the stability of plasmonic NFs. It is demonstrated that thermal conductivity of normal NF reduces with time but in plasmonic NF scenario is quite different due to versatile stability conditions. In recent attempts, several research groups reported pulsed laser ablation (PAL) synthesis of plasmonic NF in water by avoiding ligands that allow the fluid solution to remain stable even after many years. In deionized water (DI water) laser ablation posses zeta potential -Ve value exceeded 30-35 mV, which in turn enable the synthesized solution to be stable at room temperature [144,406].

As far as conventional NFs are concerned, scientific community have already developed numerous models to boost their thermal conductivity but in the case plasmonic NFs number of the proposed models is really minute. So, there is a need to devise outstanding models including models based on experimental data, to get better insights of plasmonic NF properties and successfully predict their behavior before applying them in real applications. In the last couple of years researchers applied machine learning and various neural network techniques to successfully predict the NP properties which results in designing of improved mathematical models. So, it is recommended to apply the available appropriate and significant model to predict the properties of plasmonic NFs. NPs are featured in a range of applications including solar absorber, medical equipment, food industry, cosmetic industry and lot more. The other aspect of these particle’s plentiful applications is terrible impact on our ecosystem that include from microorganism to humans. Some published works highlighted their toxic effects on human health if humans are exposed and remain in contact with these NPs for long span of time [407,408]. Some of the NPs, like Ag, even damage human health in their non-toxic form as well. They can affect membrane of microcells, mitochondria and even DNA undergo many structural and functional disorders [409,410]. These NPs also stuck in the nasopharyngeal vein upon inhaling and then further move into cerebellum via olfactory nerves [409,411]. As they possess nano-dimension, they may swiftly move into brain cells by crossing the blood brain barrier and produce drastic physiological and brain functioning affects by reducing the number of dopamine cells [412,413]. The enormous development and industrial usage of NP impart several harmful impacts on environment. The intensification in NPs-human contact is seen after domestic utilization of NPs [414,415]. Several conducted experiments in lab reveals that NPs rapidly incorporate into surrounding environment and contaminate the area in their vicinity. It has been observed that they contaminate the soil, water reservoir and even the environment around us as well. The presence of these NPs facilitates in bacterial mutation and also show direct affects on human health. These NPs even have smaller dimensions compare to pollen grains and some other allergens which prompt them to launch swift attack on human and animal immune system. The artificially or human-synthesized NPs are declared even more dangerous compared to the naturally existing nanosized particles as human and other animal’s immune systems have not yet developed their immunity against artificially synthesized NPs [416,417].

In order to improve the effectiveness and dependability of NF research, we propose the use of standardized techniques for quantifying thermal conductivity of NFs. These protocols should thoroughly address issues, such as particle concentration, temperature range, and environmental conditions to guarantee consistency and comparability across several experiments. The generation of detailed database that includes intricate characteristics of working, like particle dimensions, morphology, and resilience measurements in diverse circumstances, can greatly benefit scientists and stakeholders in the business. This source can provide convenient access to in-depth and standardized database, thus facilitating advancement of more efficient energy systems that employ NFs.

The discoveries have wide-ranging ramifications that go beyond the scientific and technological communities, including sustainability and energy policy. The NFs have the ability to greatly improve the efficiency of energy systems. By incorporating them, we can reduce energy consumption and carbon footprints in many industries. Thus, implementing the policy that support the advancement of NFs technologies, stress their incorporation into marketable goods, as well as stimulate research funding for expanding these breakthroughs will expedite the acceptance of environmentally-friendly energy solutions on a global scale. Promoting the significance of NFs in achieving energy sustainability objectives can also facilitate direction to governmental and international regulatory frameworks in promoting eco-friendly technologies.

This present study, therefore, highlights a systematic investigation of the impact of NP stability, size, and morphology on NF thermal conductivity to provide the necessary insight toward the development of reliable and scalable hybrid models. Such advantages enable enhancing predictive capability and deliverability of NFs in energy applications. The review explores various NF-related topics, emphasizing their integration into solar energy harvesting systems. It is supposed to analyze parameters affecting the collectors’ efficiency, pointing out problems and critical issues of the sector, outlining experimental validation, theoretical model refinement, and practical implementation. Primarily, it shows how NFs can improve efficiency of hybrid PV/T systems and direct absorption solar collectors. It further emphasizes the need to address health and environmental concerns attributed to the use of NPs, demanding for more studies to be undertaken to improve nano-fabrication techniques to ensure their durable sustainability. It presents an effective two-step synthesis method for NF production to ensure a highly efficient and stable NF. One important point arising from the findings indicates that only NP types compatible with base-fluids enhance the performance of NF. Of particular significance is that Fe-doped Au shells may improve solar energy absorption well, hence highlighting the potential of NFs in renewable energy applications.

NFs are expected to be stable and perform well in solar thermal applications. As mentioned, the addition of stabilizers has been found crucial, providing avoidance of NP sedimentation and agglomeration and thus ensuring NFs’ long-term stability. Besides, surface chemistry and the amount of stabilizer optimization is relevant, given stability under different environmental conditions. Advanced methods to stabilize NFs are encouraged to propose practical solar energy systems based on NFs. Otherwise, bare NPs having a negative zeta-potential of more than –30 mV, which could be obtained using a pulsed laser ablation technique, can generate intrinsically stabilized NFs. The research unravels that particles size significantly influences the thermal and optical characteristics of NFs, thus manipulating their solar energy conversion efficiency. Precise fluctuations in NP size (10 to 50 nm) effectively influence NFs optical absorbance and scattering characteristics. Fine fabrication control and accurate characterization of NPs size are essential to optimize NF-based solar collectors.

The future developments of methodologies of synthesis and manipulation of nanoparticles hold great potential to make nanofluids even more effective and versatile in renewable energy applications, especially solar technologies. The morphology of the particles significantly affects the thermal conduction, optical absorption, and scattering properties, since all these factors fundamentally influence the efficiencies of nanofluid-based solar energy systems. In particular, nanoparticles with non-spherical shapes, like ellipsoids and blades, have much higher light-trapping capabilities and higher photothermal conversion efficiency than their spherical counterparts. This should be exploited in formulating tailored NFs for renewable energy applications. Full exploitation of the benefits of non-spherical nanoparticles is foreseen to deliver highly performing nanofluids that can raise the bar in solar energy conversion efficiency significantly.

Considering solar collectors have considerable heat losses, their performance significantly improves when integrated NFs are integrated. Significant improvements have already been realized in both flat plate collectors and PV/T systems in which NFs were used, but their integration into high-temperature concentrators can be promising. NF can increase freshwater production by 30% to 57.9% in solar desalination systems.

CQDs, SWCNT, Al
2
O
3
, and Fe-doped Au shells are among the most promising working fluids for generating heat in photothermal conversion. However, there is an urgent need to address environmental and health concerns related to using NPs. Environmentally friendly NFs need to be developed. Waste substances are liberated into the natural habitat, and the accidental leak from a laboratory and application site severely threatens ecosystems and public health. Safe handling and environmentally friendly NF design urgently need to be developed. In the future, research should concentrate on further improvement of synthesizing methods of nanofluids and their applications without harming the environment and human health. One may still ask the following question: what is future of NFs, and will their promising potential continue to be realized? From the broad research reviewed here, it seems that interest and research in NFs are increasingly escalating. From both experimental and theoretical point of view, NFs are able to be fabricated from nearly any bulk material and incorporated into any fluid medium, be it liquid or gaseous. That, of course, takes the development of new methods that could overcome existing limitations to the longer-term stability and maximum efficiency of operational NFs in practically viable media, like solar thermal collectors and other heat exchangers. Based on extensive review, it is asserted that NFs will have lasting and significant impacts in numerous fields for the foreseeable future.
